coredata.dc:title: Tuning the electronic transport properties for a trigonal graphene flake
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2012 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Xiaoqing
Tang, Guiping
Guo, Chao
coredata.dc:description: By applying nonequilibrium Greenʼs functions in combination with density-functional theory, we have investigated the effects of two side groups, NH2 and NO2, on the electronic transport properties of the trigonal graphene flake. It has been found that the rectifying ratios (RR) and direction can be significantly tuned by the type and the attached positions of side groups. The NH2 group shows an obvious electron-donating characteristic, whereas NO2 group demonstrates a poorly electron-accepting behavior in these systems. The analysis on the spatial distribution and the energy level of frontier orbitals, transmission spectra, and electrostatic potential distribution give an inside view of the observed results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electronic transport
Graphene quantum dot
Density-functional theory

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The trigonal graphene flake is sandwiched between two Au(111) electrodes. The side groups, R1 and R2, connecting the trigonal graphene flake are listed in Table 1.
(a) The I–V characteristics and (b) rectifying ratio changes with bias for all models.
The transmission spectra of models (a) A1 and A2, (b) B1 and B2, (c) C1 and C2 under various biases of 0, ±1.4, and ±2.8 V, respectively, the region between two dash lines indicates the bias window.
Energy levels as functions of the applied bias: (a) for model B1 and (b) for model B2. The black solid lines mark the left and right limits of the bias window.
The electrostatic potential distribution of models A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, and C2.
The models in this work, R1 and R2, are the connecting side groups.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Net electrons denote the self-consistently calculated Mulliken charge distributions of side groups for our models at zero bias, and the sign “−” denotes the electron-donating behavior.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spatial distribution for the HOMO and LUMO states in all models at zero bias, and also for the HOMO state at ±2.8 V.
The Mulliken charge of each atom Se for all modes at zero bias.

head.ce:title: Tuning the electronic transport properties for a trigonal graphene flake

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronic Science, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha 410004, Peopleʼs Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By applying nonequilibrium Greenʼs functions in combination with density-functional theory, we have investigated the effects of two side groups, NH2 and NO2, on the electronic transport properties of the trigonal graphene flake. It has been found that the rectifying ratios (RR) and direction can be significantly tuned by the type and the attached positions of side groups. The NH2 group shows an obvious electron-donating characteristic, whereas NO2 group demonstrates a poorly electron-accepting behavior in these systems. The analysis on the spatial distribution and the energy level of frontier orbitals, transmission spectra, and electrostatic potential distribution give an inside view of the observed results.

► We have investigated electronic transport properties of the trigonal graphene flake attached by a pair of NH2 (NO2) groups. ► The transportation of these contact-asymmetrical junctions show various rectifying behaviors. ► The rectifying ratios (RR) and directions can be significantly tuned by the type and the connected positions of side groups.

ce:section.ce:para:
Graphene, a two-dimensional (2D) single-atom thick sheet of s
p
2
hybridized carbon atoms packed into hexagonal structure, continues to attract immense interest due to its unusual mechanical [1,2], thermal [3,4], electrical properties [5], and spintronics [6]. Enormous efforts have been devoted to investigate its electronic properties for its massless Dirac Fermion with high mobility and good mutual conductance [7–9]. However, the pristine graphene is a kind of gapless material, which limits its electronics applications because semiconductor nanoscale materials with tunable band gap are more desirable from the perspective of potential nanoelectronics applications [10]. The geometry and chemistry methods are considered to be two main aspects for graphene functionalizations. The chemical decoration can convert zigzag graphene nanoribbons (ZGNRs) to half metal [11], and it is also possible to transform a highly-conductive semimetal into an insulator by exposing graphene to the atomic hydrogen [12]. The electronic properties of graphene also can be controlled by doping, which is a process intentionally used to tailor the electrical properties of intrinsic semiconductors. The patterning of segregated BN nanodomains embedded into a graphene layer was observed in laser vaporization grown C–BN single-wall nanotubes [13]. One-dimensional GNRs and zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which can be obtained through the geometry cutting method provide a possibility to explore low-dimensional transport properties for carbon-based nanoelectronics. The trigonal graphene flake, a kind of representative zero-dimensional GQDs with symmetry group of C3V, is prominent in electronic and magnetic properties because there exist N-fold degenerated half-filled zero-energy states when its size is N [14]. Very recently, experimental observations and theoretical studies show that triangular GQDs with zigzag edges have degenerate zero-energy states and show metallic ferromagnetism, and the energy gaps for GQDs in the graphane can be controlled by the size [15]. These special properties render GQD as the building block for nanodevices such as spin memory, transistors, solid-state qubits, and perhaps optoelectronics applications [16,17].

In order to enrich the potential applications of graphene, we explore electronic transport properties of the trigonal zigzag flake, and focus particularly on the altering effect of rectifying behaviors when both NO2 and NH2 groups are attached to the left or/and right side(s) of a trigonal graphene flake and the influence of their relative positions through an analysis on the frontier molecular orbitals and the charge distribution in the molecule. It has been found that the type of side groups and their relative locations have obvious influences on the electronic transport properties of these devices.

The molecular architectures used in our study are shown in Fig. 1 . The trigonal graphene flake is bridged between two Au ( 111 ) electrodes through Se atoms. A previous study showed that benzeneselenolate (C6H5Se) can be adsorbed on the Au ( 111 ) surface by forming stable AuSe bonds. The molecule–electrode contact distance is set as 2.7 Å, which is in the range from 2.6 to 3.2 Å used by researchers [18]. To construct a molecular junction, each optimized molecule (SemoleculeSe) is translated into two ( 3 × 3 ) Au ( 111 ) electrodes. The first two gold layers which are close to the central molecule are included in the self-consistent cycle to take into account the molecule–electrode coupling and the electrode screening effect, while the remainder of the electrodes is described by bulk parameters. The definition for all of models according to the side group type and their position can be found in Table 1 : Model A1 (A2) corresponds to only double amino (nitro) groups attached at the left side of molecule. When the double amino (nitro) groups are attached at the left (right) or left (right) side, the model B1 or B2 is obtained. Model C1 (C2) corresponds to only double amino (nitro) groups attached at the right side of molecule.

The theoretical calculations of electron transport are carried out using the ATOMISTIX TOOLKIT (ATK) software [19–21] based on density-functional theory (DFT) combined with the nonequilibrium Greenʼs function (NEGF) formalism. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) schemes are used with Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) correlation functional. Core electrons are modeled with Troullier–Martins nonlocal pseudopotential, and valence electrons are expanded in a SIESTA localized basis set. An energy cut off of 200 Ry is used to define the real-space grid for numerical calculations in the electron density. Single-zeta (SZ) basis set for Au atoms and double-zeta plus polarization (DZP) basis set for other atoms are adopted. The geometries are optimized until all residual forces on each atom are smaller than 0.05 eV / Å .

The transmittance spectra under the external applied bias V are calculated using the well-known formula,
(1)
T ( E , V ) = Tr
[
Γ
L
( E , V )
G
C
+
( E , V )
Γ
R
( E , V )
G
C
−
( E , V ) ]
,
where
G
C
±
are the advanced and retarded Greenʼs functions of the conductor,
Γ
L , R
are the coupling functions of the conductor to the left and right leads. The trace runs over all orbitals and the coupling
Γ
L , R
is twice the imaginary part of the self-energy matrix
Σ
L ( R )
,
Σ
L ( R )
is used to include the effect of the left (right) semi-infinite electrode, which is explicitly included in the Kohn–Sham calculation for the extended molecule system. It can be determined by the Greenʼs function technique,
(2)
Σ
L ( R )
=
C
L ( R )
+
G
L ( R )
C
L ( R )
,
where
C
L ( R )
is the coupling matrix of the molecule to the left (right) metallic electrode and
G
L ( R )
is the Greenʼs function of the semi-infinite electrode. The current I in a molecular–metal system as a function of the applied external bias V can be calculated from the Landauer–Büttiker formula [22,23],
(3)
I =
G
0
∫
u
L
u
R
T ( E , V )
d E ,
where
G
0
= 2
e
2
/ h is the quantum unit of conductance.
μ
L
(
μ
R
) is the chemical potential of the left (right) electrode:
μ
L
=
E
f
+
V 2
,
μ
R
=
E
f
−
V 2
, where
E
f
is average Fermi level, the average value of the chemical potential of the left and right electrodes. The transport coefficient T ( E , V ) is a function of the energy level E at a certain bias V. [
μ
L
,
μ
R
] is an energy region which contributes to the current integral and is called as the bias window or integral window. Considering the fact that the Fermi level is set to zero, the region of the bias window is factually [ − V / 2 , + V / 2 ] .

The I – V characteristics for six models in a wide bias region from −2.8 to 2.8
V are shown in Fig. 2 (a), where the curves are asymmetric with respect to the voltage inversion. In the low bias range, the currents vary approximately linearly, corresponding to a non-resonant tunneling. By increasing the bias further, the current raises significantly and changes nonlinearly. For models A2 and B2, the onset of a larger current occurs at the lower positive bias, thus there is a larger current in the positive bias regime than in the negative bias regime. At the bias of − 1.6 V , the current increases sharply and becomes larger than that at positive bias. However, the variation of currents in models A1, B1, C1, and C2 shows a different trend compared with models A2 and B2. A significant rectification in current can be observed in Fig. 2(b), where the rectification ratio is defined as R ( V ) = I ( V ) / | I ( − V ) | , and I ( V ) and I ( − V ) are the currents under forward and reverse biases, respectively. We define that the rectification ratio R ( V ) > 1
( R ( V ) < 1 ) is the forward (reverse) rectification. Both models A1 and B1 show a similar rectification effect under low bias and reach R ( V ) > 1 when the bias is higher than 1.8 V. This means that a reverse rectification occurs under low bias. Such a bias induced a change of rectification effect could be attributed to the bias induced re-polarization of the molecules [24]. The rectification of models C1 and C2 has a similar trend: it is a reverse rectification when the bias is lesser than 1.0 V, then a forward rectification ratio increases continuously with bias. Model C1 exhibits a best rectifying performance among all studied models. For models A2 and B2, however, their rectification behaviors show an obvious similarity in the whole bias region: a forward rectification can be observed when the bias is lesser than 2.0 V, then reverse rectification, and finally the ratio reaches a steadily smaller value, 0.7, with an increase of bias. The largest forward rectification ratios for six models are 4, 3.4, 3.4, 3.3, 6.5, and 4.7 under certain biases respectively.

The above results can be interpreted in terms of their transmission spectra [24,25]. The position of transmission peak is generally determined by the energy level of molecular orbitals, i.e. the transmission channels, and the delocalization degree of orbital affects the height of transmission peak. The transmission spectrum change when a voltage is applied since a molecule is polarized by the field, and this polarization may be asymmetric to induce a rectification in the I – V curves. We compare the transmission amplitude of models
A 1
/
A 2
,
B 1
/
B 2
,
C 1
/
C 2
at various biases of 0, ±1.4, and ± 2.8
V , as shown in Fig. 3 (a)–(c). The calculated results display an obvious dependence of the transmission spectra on the applied bias. In the energy region considered here for all models, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) plays the major role in the molecular rectification, which shows the different shift tendency in the positive and negative bias window [26–28]. Not only the shape of the transmission spectrum but also the location of transmission peak change obviously with bias, leading to different currents in terms of the Landauer–Büttiker formula. The approximate coincidence of the current–voltage curve of A2 and B2 originates from the significant similarity in transmission spectra. Especially when the bias is larger than − 1.4 V , both the HOMO and LUMO+1 transmission peaks are driven into the bias window, leading to a sharp increase of current (seen in Fig. 2), where the LUMO transmission peak becomes very weak for a highly localized LUMO channel. The origin of the rectification properties can be understood from the position and intensity of transmission peaks in the bias window. Typically, for model C1 in Fig. 3(c), with bias increasing, the transmission peak of HOMO resonance rises at positive bias, but keep unchanged at negative bias with a low value. The integral area of transmission coefficient within the bias windows is distinctly larger at the positive bias than that at the same negative bias. Therefore, a strong forward rectification can be expected as a result of this asymmetry when the bias is larger than 1.4 V. When the voltage further increases to 2.8 V, the HOMO peak with high intensity and broad energy region enters into the bias window completely at positive bias, leading to a largest rectification ratio.

The transmission coefficient can be related to the molecular orbitals which have been modified by the electrodes, and these modified molecular orbitals can be obtained from the molecular projected self-consistent Hamiltonian (MPSH), where the self-consistent of molecular junction is projected onto the molecule, and this MPSH matrix is diagonalized [29,30]. Table 2 illustrates the spatial distributions of the eigenstates of MPSH for the HOMO and LUMO in models A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, and C2 at zero bias, and also for the HOMO state at ± 2.8 V . It can be seen that both HOMO and LUMO states tend to be obviously localized, which contributes to asymmetry of current–voltage ( I – V ) characteristics. The HOMO state, an important channel for electron transmission channel, distributes largely in the left region of a device in models A1, B1, C1, and C2, namely, more overlaps of the orbitals between a molecule and the left electrode happen. Under the positive bias, the electrons are easier to transfer from the molecule to the left electrode through the left contact, and are more difficult to be injected from the right electrode into the molecule through the right contact, so the molecule loses charges, which moves HOMO to lower energies. For models A2 and B2, under negative bias, the HOMO state distributes largely in the right region of a device, the electrons are easier to transfer from the molecule to the right electrode, but more difficult to inject from the left electrode into molecule, so the molecules also lose charge, which moves HOMO to lower energies. In fact, the bias also affects the distribution of HOMO, which is fully delocalized on the whole molecule at 2.8 V and provides a good channel for the electron transport, leading to the high HOMO resonance peaks for models A1, B1, C1, and C2 at such a bias. However, at − 2.8 V , the HOMO still localizes on the left side, the HOMO resonant peak therefore possesses a small value, as shown in Fig. 2. While for models A2 and B2, the HOMO distribution keeps steady at various biases. We also calculate the self-consistently Mulliken charge distribution of side groups in these models at zero bias, as shown in Table 1. It is interesting to note that the NH2 group shows an obvious electron-donating behavior, but NO2 group shows a little electron-accepting behavior. The transfer of charge plays a major role in the change of the electron structure and potential distribution due to the intramolecular charge redistribution.

The shift of molecular orbitals with respect to the Fermi level under bias is an important factor dominating the junction transportation. Fig. 4 shows the evolution of levels between the LUMO+3 and HOMO−3 with bias for models B1 and B2, where the Fermi level of the systems is set to be zero. It can be seen that these levels show different shifting tendency in both positive and negative bias windows, which should affect the rectification ratio under various biases. We can see that energy levels of model B1 shift more obviously under positive bias than under negative bias, which is consistent with Fig. 3(b), leading to an asymmetrical variation of the HOMO–LUMO gap. Under lower bias, there are more levels entering into bias window at negative bias compared with positive bias. However, the levels have different sifting trend in model B2, both the HOMO and LUMO levels shift to lower energies under negative bias, and the HOMO position at positive bias shows a variation with the applied biases but the change is relatively small and enters into the bias window earlier than that at negative bias. Specially, the LUMO level moves down further and passes over the Fermi level when the bias is larger than 1.8 V. In comparison with Fig. 3(b), the first larger transmission peak above the Fermi level for model B2 originates from the LUMO+1 level at the zero bias.

The distribution of electrostatic potential for models A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, and C2 are shown in Fig. 5 . Electrons transfer from electrodes to the molecule core leading to the higher electrostatic potential in a central region. Two Schottky barriers at both interfaces are formed, and the size of both Schottky barriers is significant asymmetric due to asymmetric coupling of the molecule to two electrodes. The total Schottky barrier in devices is similar in models A1, B1, C1, and C2. The left Schottky barrier which is higher than the right one is reduced under negative bias to cause the current rising. However, under positive bias, the total Schottky barrier increases leading to that electrons move difficultly. Thus current is small and we can observe the reverse rectification effect at low bias. For models A2 and B2, the potential gradient at the left side is lower than that at the right side, therefore a reverse rectification effect can be observed in these two models. We also analyze the potential barriers by calculating the contribution from Mulliken charge of each atom Se in these models at zero bias, as shown in Table 3 . The right Se atoms in all models and the left Se atoms in models A1, B1, C1, and C2 are positively charged. Since there are more electrons located on the site of the left Se atom, an obvious potential barrier is established between the phenyl and the Au surface at the left side for models A1, B1, C1, and C2. Furthermore, there is depletion of electrons on the left Se atom site for models A2 and B2, which shows that there is no potential barrier between the phenyl and the Au surface at the left side. This deficiency of electrons of atoms provides a potential channel for the transfer of electrons from the molecule to the surface of Au or from the surface of Au to the molecule [30]. We can see that the O atoms of the two NO2 groups seem very close to the Se atom in models A2 and B2, so the electron repulsion effects lead to the variety of charge transfer phenomena according to point of Stadler [31]. Especially interfacial Pauli repulsion leads to the relatively small amount of charging in the left interface between electrode and molecule.

In summary, using the nonequilibrium Greenʼs function method combined with the density-functional theory, the transport properties of trigonal graphene flake attached by side groups connected to two symmetric Au electrodes are investigated. The strong AuSe coupling between electrodes and the molecule induces more charge transfer across the metal–molecule interface to form the two Schottky barriers at both interfaces. The size of both Schottky barriers is significant asymmetric due to an asymmetric coupling of a molecule to two electrodes and it can be regulated by the attached side groups. When the NH2 or NO2 group is connected onto the left side of molecule, the rectifying direction is obviously opposite. We show the largest rectification ratio is close to 4, 3.4, 3.4, 3.3, 6.5, and 6 under certain biases for six models. It suggests that the rectifying ratio and direction are closely related with the type and the connected position of side groups.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 61071015 and 61101009), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (Grant Nos. 11B008 and 11C0066) and the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Agency (Grant No. 2011FJ3089).
